mission: Greet your speaker in Georgian, introduce yourself with 'მე ვარ ...', and ask how they are. Report back how they answered.
Ask your speaker their name and where they are from, even if you already know — it is practice! Report back their answers in Georgian.
Ask whether your speaker has brothers or sisters and how many. Report back using მყავს/ჰყავს forms.

phrases.en: Do you have a brother or a sister?
How many children do you have?

mission: Find out your speaker's favorite Georgian dish and tell them one food you love using მიყვარს. Report back what they said.
Ask about the size of your speaker's family and where everyone lives. Report back at least two facts you learned.

phrases.en: Do you have a big family?
Who are the members of your family?

mission: Ask what your speaker loves doing in their free time and share one hobby of yours. Report back their favorite activity.
Tell your speaker you want to learn Georgian well and ask whether they think it is hard. Report back their encouragement (or warnings!).

phrases.en: I want to learn Georgian well.

mission: Point at three objects around you and ask 'ეს რა არის?' for each. Report back the three new words you collected.

phrases.en: How would it be in Georgian?

theyMayAsk.en: Do you know what this is?

mission: Ask where your speaker works and whether they like their job. Report back one detail about their work.

theyMayAsk.en: What do you do at work?

mission: Ask about your speaker's morning routine — when they get up and what they drink. Report back their schedule.

phrases.en: When do you get up in the morning?
I get up at seven o'clock.
Do you drink coffee in the morning?

theyMayAsk.en: Do you like coffee or tea?

mission: Ask where your speaker buys bread and whether the shop is near their home. Report back using a postposition like -თან or ახლოს.

theyMayAsk.en: Do you go to the market?

mission: Ask your speaker to teach you one new word, and ask them to repeat it until you can say it. Report back the word and its meaning.
Ask the names of two of your speaker's family members using genitive forms. Report back with phrases like 'მისი დის სახელია ...'.

theyMayAsk.en: This is my grandchild, isn't he/she pretty?

mission: Ask your speaker how their day is going and whether they are tired. Report back one thing happening in their day.
Ask what your speaker will do this weekend and share one plan of yours in the future tense. Report back their plans.

phrases.en: What will you do on the weekend?
I am planning to go for a walk.
Where will you go in the summer?

theyMayAsk.en: Will you come to our place?

mission: Invite your speaker for coffee or a walk next week and agree on a time. Report back when you will meet.

phrases.en: Will you come for coffee next week?

mission: Ask your speaker whom in the family you should address as შენ and whom as თქვენ. Report back the rules they gave you.

phrases.en: How should I address you — shen or tkven?
Should I say tkven to grandma?
I always say tkven to strangers.

theyMayAsk.en: Does this distinction exist in English?

mission: Ask what your speaker ate yesterday. Report back in the third person with 'მან ჭამა ...'.
Ask what your speaker did last weekend and where they went. Report back two things using the aorist.

phrases.en: What did you do on the weekend?

mission: Ask where your speaker was born and when they came to their current city. Report back a mini biography in two sentences.

phrases.en: When did you move to Tbilisi?
Where did you live in your childhood?

mission: Ask your speaker how a favorite dish is made and what is needed for it. Report back at least two steps of the recipe.

phrases.en: Will you teach me how to make it?

theyMayAsk.en: Do you want us to cook it together?

mission: Ask your speaker what you should see in Georgia and where you should go first. Report back their top recommendation.

phrases.en: What should I see in Georgia?
Is it worth going to Kakheti?

theyMayAsk.en: Do you like the mountains or the sea?
How many days do you have?

mission: Call your speaker on the phone (or simulate a call) and manage the greeting, one question, and the goodbye entirely in Georgian. Report back how long you lasted.

phrases.en: I will call you back later.
Sorry, the sound is not coming through well.

theyMayAsk.en: Can you speak a bit louder?

mission: Ask what your speaker would do if they won a million. Share what you would do using the conditional. Report back their answer.

phrases.en: If you won a million, what would you do?
I would travel the whole world.
Would you live in another country?

mission: Ask whether your speaker has ever been abroad and where. Report back with perfect forms like 'ის ყოფილა ...'.

theyMayAsk.en: What have you seen in Georgia?

mission: Ask how your speaker is feeling and what makes them happiest. Report back using indirect verbs like უხარია.

phrases.en: I am happy when I see family.

mission: Ask your speaker's opinion: is life better in the city or in the village, and why. Report back their argument in one sentence.

phrases.ka: თქვენი აზრით, სად ჯობია ცხოვრება — ქალაქში თუ სოფელში?
phrases.en: In your opinion, where is life better — in the city or in the village?

theyMayAsk.en: How is it in your country?

mission: Ask whether your speaker has ever tried a dish from your country and offer to make it. Report back their answer with perfect forms.

phrases.en: I have not yet tried chacha.

mission: Prepare a short toast and deliver it at the table (or over tea). Report back how the family reacted.

phrases.en: I want to say a toast.
And here's to you! (reply to a toast)

theyMayAsk.en: You will say a toast, won't you?
Do you know what a tamada is?

mission: Ask what your speaker is most proud of, and thank them for their patience with your Georgian. Report back what they said — this is your graduation mission.

phrases.en: What are you most proud of?
I have learned a lot from you.
